filing_and_agency_information.from_public_agency: City of Sacramento Department of Public Works

project_information.project_title: Midtown Alley Pedestrian Lighting and Safety Improvements
project_information.project_applicant: City of Sacramento Department of Public Works
project_information.project_location_specific: Public alley between 20th Street and 21st Street, from J Street to K Street, Sacramento, CA 95816 (APNs 007-0153-012, -013, -017, and -018)
project_information.description_of_nature_purpose_and_benefi:
The project consists of minor public improvements to an existing paved alley in the Midtown neighborhood of Sacramento. Specifically, the City will install approximately twelve (12) new LED pedestrian-scale light standards on existing utility poles and on two new 12-foot decorative poles, upgrade two existing light fixtures to energy-efficient LED heads, add four (4) curb ramps at existing alley intersections to comply with current ADA standards, and restripe existing alley pavement to clearly delineate pedestrian walking areas and loading zones. No widening, grading, or expansion of the alley is proposed beyond its existing footprint.

The primary purpose of the project is to improve nighttime visibility, pedestrian safety, and accessibility in a heavily used alley that serves multiple mixed-use buildings, small businesses, and multi-family residential units. Over the past several years, local residents and business owners have raised concerns about inadequate lighting, tripping hazards at the alley intersections, and conflicts between delivery vehicles, rideshare pickups, and pedestrians walking through the alley during evening and nighttime hours. Sacramento Police Department collision and call-for-service data show several minor incidents and near-misses in the project area, particularly on weekend evenings when nearby restaurants and bars are busiest.

Beneficiaries of the project include:
- Residents of the adjacent apartment buildings and condominiums who regularly use the alley as a primary walking route to nearby transit stops, grocery stores, and neighborhood amenities.
- Customers and employees of the small businesses fronting the alley, including a coffee shop, two restaurants, a bookstore, and a small creative office space, who rely on the alley for safe access, deliveries, and trash collection.
- People with disabilities and older adults who will benefit from the installation of accessible curb ramps and smoother transitions between the sidewalk and alley, reducing the risk of falls and making mobility device use easier.
- The broader Midtown community and visitors, who will experience improved perceptions of safety, reduced dark spots, and a clearer separation between pedestrian space and vehicle loading areas.

Construction activities will be limited in scope and duration, consisting primarily of trenching within existing paved surfaces for short utility runs, installation of small concrete pole foundations within existing paved or previously disturbed areas, mounting of fixtures on existing poles, saw-cutting and pouring of new curb ramps within existing sidewalk and alley edges, and application of new striping and pavement markings. Work will occur during normal daytime construction hours and will use standard best management practices for dust control, noise minimization, and traffic control. No trees will be removed, no new impervious areas will be created, and no expansion of use beyond existing conditions will occur. The project is designed to enhance safety and accessibility while maintaining the existing character and intensity of use of the alley.
project_information.name_of_person_or_agency_carrying_out_pr: City of Sacramento Department of Public Works, Engineering Services Division

exemption_status.categorical_exemption_state_type_and_sec: Class 1, Existing Facilities (CEQA Guidelines §15301(c)); Class 3, New Construction or Conversion of Small Structures (CEQA Guidelines §15303(d)); and Class 4, Minor Alterations to Land (CEQA Guidelines §15304(f))
exemption_status.reasons_why_project_is_exempt:
The project qualifies for a categorical exemption under multiple sections of the State CEQA Guidelines because it involves minor alterations to existing public facilities within an urbanized area, with no expansion of use and no potential for significant environmental impacts.

1. Class 1 – Existing Facilities (CEQA Guidelines §15301(c)):
The proposed improvements will occur entirely within the footprint of existing public right-of-way (an already paved alley and adjacent sidewalks) in a fully developed, urban neighborhood of Sacramento. The work consists of minor alterations to existing streets and sidewalks, including the installation of pedestrian-scale lighting on existing utility poles where feasible, the replacement of existing light fixtures with more efficient LED units, the construction of ADA-compliant curb ramps at existing intersections, and restriping of existing paved surfaces to better organize vehicular and pedestrian use. There will be no increase in capacity of the alley, no new through lanes, and no change in the basic function of the facility. The alley will continue to serve the same residential and commercial properties at the same intensity of use as under existing conditions. These activities are squarely within the scope of Class 1 exemptions for existing facilities involving negligible or no expansion of use.

2. Class 3 – New Construction or Conversion of Small Structures (CEQA Guidelines §15303(d)):
The project also qualifies under Class 3 because it involves the installation of a limited number of small, new appurtenant structures (pedestrian-scale light poles and related minor utility connections) within an existing developed alley. The new poles are modest in height (approximately 12 feet), will occupy very small footprints, and are similar in size and scale to existing urban streetscape furnishings such as sign posts, utility poles, and bollards. No substantial grading, building construction, or expansion of floor area is involved. The lighting equipment and electrical conduits are typical small utility extensions normally associated with urban infrastructure and are therefore consistent with Class 3(d).

3. Class 4 – Minor Alterations to Land (CEQA Guidelines §15304(f)):
The minor trenching required for underground conduit, as well as the saw-cutting and reconstruction of curb ramps, will occur within existing paved or previously disturbed surfaces. Any ground disturbance is shallow and limited in extent, and all disturbed areas will be restored to their previous condition with new pavement or concrete. No native vegetation, sensitive habitat, or undisturbed soils will be affected. As such, the work constitutes minor alterations to land that do not involve the removal of healthy, mature, or scenic trees and meets the criteria for Class 4(f).

4. Absence of Unusual Circumstances and Other Exceptions:
Staff has reviewed the project relative to the exceptions to the use of categorical exemptions in CEQA Guidelines §15300.2 and determined that none of the exceptions apply:
- Location: The project is not located in a particularly sensitive environment such as a wetland, riparian corridor, or designated habitat area. It is within an already urbanized, paved alley surrounded by existing buildings and streets.
- Cumulative impacts: The project is a small, stand-alone improvement and is not part of a larger program of related projects that, together, would result in significant cumulative impacts. Similar alley lighting upgrades in other parts of the city have not resulted in significant environmental effects.
- Unusual circumstances: The project involves typical urban infrastructure upgrades (lighting, curb ramps, striping) that are common in cities throughout California. There are no unusual features or conditions that would create a reasonable possibility of significant environmental impacts.
- Scenic resources: The project is not located within a state scenic highway corridor, and no protected scenic vistas or resources will be affected. The added lighting is modest in scale and designed to minimize glare and light spill.
- Hazardous waste sites: The project is not located on or adjacent to a site listed on any of the hazardous waste or hazardous materials lists compiled under Government Code §65962.5.
- Historical resources: The alley itself is not a designated historic resource, and the project does not involve demolition or substantial alteration of any buildings. Lighting and curb ramp improvements are reversible and compatible with the existing urban context.

Given the limited scope of work, its location within existing disturbed right-of-way, and the absence of any reasonably foreseeable significant environmental effects, the project is categorically exempt from CEQA under the cited sections.

applicant_filing_and_certification.title: Senior Planner, City of Sacramento Department of Public Works
